title: Podman Containers (Easy to understand) Docs
body:
### Feature request description

According to the Most frequent queries asked during tech conferences, peopletend to have little to no understanding of containerisation and the problems Podman solves. We currently have a basic solution of a coloring book [here](https://developers.redhat.com/e-books/container-commandos-coloring-book) and a verbose docs collection [here](https://podman.io/docs) and [here](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/), but something to bridge the gap between these granularity of verbosity should be made available.


### Suggest potential solution

A form a documentation which makes it easy from a novice programmer to start implementing on their use case with zero learning curve needs to exist.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

_No response_

### Additional context

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

comments.body:
I wrote this Podman guide for beginners
https://github.com/eriksjolund/using-podman-quick-instructions
a few years ago. Some parts of it might be useful.
(I could add a LICENSE file with an open source license)

> I wrote this Podman guide for beginners https://github.com/eriksjolund/using-podman-quick-instructions a few years ago. Some parts of it might be useful. (I could add a LICENSE file with an open source license)

Thanks @eriksjolund !
Would you want to contribute this to Podman.io?

Sure, that would be cool. I just added a Apache-2.0 license to the repository.
Should I could create a PR and add it under
https://github.com/containers/podman/tree/main/docs/tutorials
?
But before doing that I would like to clean it up and improve it a bit.
@eriksjolund if you need any help with this issue, please feel free to leave a comment, thanks!

> @eriksjolund if you need any help with this issue, please feel free to leave a comment, thanks!

@cgiradkar Thanks! I have had little time over for this. I hope the slow progress is not blocking the documentation effort by others. I would just be happy for any reuse of the text from the document [using-podman-quick-instructions](https://github.com/eriksjolund/using-podman-quick-instructions) under the given license (Apache-2.0). No need to keep the document as one single unit. The text can also be reused in smaller parts.

I also want to say that I plan to rework and polish up the document but I should probably not give a time forecast for it. 
One thing I would like to explore is the use of tabs 
https://docusaurus.io/docs/markdown-features/tabs#displaying-a-default-tab
because the document got to be a bit lengthy. I documented the same thing for different container images (Fedora, , CentOS, Alpine, Ubuntu).
Thanks @eriksjolund for the reference and suggestion. Will take this up soon.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@rhatdan  I will take this up next.

Planned parts of th story as discussed with @Luap99 (inspired by [docker](https://docs.docker.com/get-started/) getting started):

1. Overview
    - What is a container?
    - What is an image?
2. [Containerize an application](https://developers.redhat.com/topics/containers)
3. Update the application ([podman-stop ](https://docs.podman.io/en/v4.4/markdown/podman-stop.1.html)& [podman-rm](https://docs.podman.io/en/stable/markdown/podman-rm.1.html))
4. [Share the application](https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/share-container-image-podman-save)
5. [Persist the DB](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/volume.html)
6. Use bind mounts (need info)
7. [Multi container apps](https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/multi-container-application-podman-quadlet)
8. Use Docker Compose ([article](https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/podman-docker-compose) by Brent)
9. [Image-building best practices](https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2021/11/11/best-practices-building-images-pass-red-hat-container-certification?source=sso#best_practice__4__build_images_in_multiple_stages)


@rhatdan @Luap99 plz suggest changes if any.

> 6. Use bind mounts (need info)

One useful thing to know about bind mounts is that  some container images require the 
command-line option `--userns=keep-id:uid=$uid,gid=$gid` when bind-mounting a directory that only the regular
user on the host has access to.
Finding out how to set  $uid and $gid for the command-line option `--userns=keep-id:uid=$uid,gid=$gid` can be a bit tricky.
I tried to automate the detection of $uid and $gid with some Bash shell scripts. The result for some popular container images can be seen here:
https://github.com/eriksjolund/podman-detect-option#result-from-some-popular-container-images
(This is still a bit untested but the list of UIDs and GIDs might be useful information already)

> > 6. Use bind mounts (need info)
> 
> One useful thing to know about bind mounts is that some container images require the command-line option `--userns=keep-id:uid=$uid,gid=$gid` when bind-mounting a directory that only the regular user on the host has access to. Finding out how to set $uid and $gid for the command-line option `--userns=keep-id:uid=$uid,gid=$gid` can be a bit tricky. I tried to automate the detection of $uid and $gid with some Bash shell scripts. The result for some popular container images can be seen here: https://github.com/eriksjolund/podman-detect-option#result-from-some-popular-container-images (This is still a bit untested but the list of UIDs and GIDs might be useful information already)

Thanks @eriksjolund, I'll consult on testing the above!

Is this issue stale?  If not, I'm a little confused because the request seems to be for help communicating "why podman", not "how to podman".  (Is that correct @cgiradkar ?)

[This RedHat article](https://www.redhat.com/en/topics/containers/what-is-podman) seems to answer the "why" question pretty well, particularly the "Why Podman?" section.  The homepage at https://podman.io/ also has some great high-level bullet points right below the fold.

Is there still a need for this documentation / Github issue?  If so, it would help to better identify this target audience --what they know (or don't), what their typical background / context is, and what problem *they* are trying to solve.  (Why are they talking to you?  What are they hoping to achieve?)


If instead we're actually trying to teach people how to use Podman, what points about https://podman.io/docs are too complex?

I think that the article bridges the gap. As far as I can remember, one of the motivations for this task was to consolidate the docs which are spread across multiple sources. And that article added yet another source of truth.
